risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Stratification details will be provided in a de-identified form to an independent statistician located offshore and not otherwise involved in the study for randomisation within strata, this will be generated using random number generating software[29]." Comment: The study explicitly states that the allocation sequence was generated using random number generating software, with stratified randomisation performed by an independent statistician. This is a clearly random method of sequence generation.

Quote: "Stratification details will be provided in a de-identified form to an independent statistician located offshore and not otherwise involved in the study for randomisation within strata... the sequence will be concealed until the intervention is assigned. Thus, generation of the allocation sequence and assigning of ASUs to either intervention or control group will be undertaken by the offshore independent statistician." Comment: This explicitly describes concealment of the cluster allocation sequence from those enrolling participating ASUs, with assignment controlled by an independent statistician, preventing foreknowledge of the upcoming allocation.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Both the medical director and NUM of all consenting ASUs will be aware that our study is examining the effect of an intervention to manage fever, hyperglycaemia and swallowing dysfunction following acute stroke. Furthermore, as control ASUs receive a minimum intervention, medical directors and NUMs from ASUs subsequently randomised to the control group may be able to deduce their group allocation because no workshops are being organised." Comment: This is positive evidence that key personnel were not blinded or could infer allocation. Because the intervention consisted of workshops, education, support, and protocol implementation delivered at unit level, personnel knowledge of assignment could plausibly affect study outcomes, especially clinician behaviour/process measures and patient outcomes influenced by care delivery (e.g. disability/dependency and self-reported health status). Although patients were said to be blinded, lack of blinding of personnel in this implementation trial creates a high risk of performance bias overall.

Quote: "All CATIs will be undertaken by research interviewers blind to the study design and also to ASU group allocation"; "Patients will be blinded to group allocation."; "Data will be obtained by retrospective medical record audit undertaken by independent research assistants (IRAs) blind to group allocation." Comment: Outcome assessors included blinded research interviewers for 90-day patient outcomes, patients themselves for self-reported SF-36 (with patients stated to be blinded to allocation), and blinded independent chart abstractors for professional behaviour outcomes. Although some outcomes are subjective (mRS, Barthel Index, SF-36), the relevant assessors were reported as blinded, so knowledge of allocation was unlikely to bias outcome measurement.

Quote: "Our 90-day follow-up will comprise a computer-assisted telephone interview (CATI)." and "For missing data, patient clinical data will be obtained from the TASC database." and "Assuming a loss to follow-up of 10%, an effective sample size of 280 participants per group is required to be recruited." Comment: This is a study protocol describing planned follow-up and an anticipated 10% loss, but it does not report actual post-randomization numbers analysed at 90 days by arm, nor reasons for missing outcome data. Because observed attrition on the outcomes of interest cannot be calculated, risk of attrition bias is unclear rather than high.
